decisions.question: What backend framework should we use?
decisions.notes: Team evaluated FastAPI vs Django. Django felt too heavy for a simple API — we don't need the admin, ORM migrations are overkill at this stage. FastAPI's async-first model fits our planned architecture. Closed after first endpoint shipped and working in production.

options.label: Django REST Framework
options.rejection_reason: Heavier than needed; team unfamiliar with Django patterns; async support is bolted-on

assumptions.text: Team is comfortable with Python async/await
We will not need Django admin or built-in auth

affects.note: All route definitions, middleware, and app startup

decisions.question: How should we handle user authentication?
decisions.notes: Considered session cookies vs JWT. Chose JWT because frontend is a separate SPA and we may add a mobile client later. Implementation started but token refresh logic not yet complete — keeping open until full auth flow is tested end-to-end.

options.label: Server-side sessions with cookies
options.rejection_reason: Requires sticky sessions or shared session store; complicates future mobile client

assumptions.text: Refresh endpoint will respond fast enough for silent refresh (<300ms p95)
We will not need server-side token invalidation (e.g. forced logout)

affects.note: Token issuance and validation live here
Auth middleware validates tokens on every request

decisions.question: Should we add a Redis cache layer for task queries?
decisions.notes: Initial profiling showed task list query at ~80ms. Added Redis caching targeting <20ms. 2026-05-15: Load testing revealed query latency was actually dominated by network round-trips in the test environment, not the query itself. On real hardware the query runs at ~12ms without cache. The performance assumption was based on flawed test data. Revisiting whether cache adds enough value to justify the operational overhead.

options.label: Redis cache with 60s TTL on task list queries
No cache, optimize query with indexes
options.rejection_reason: Profiling showed indexes alone would not reach <20ms target

assumptions.text: Task list query is slow enough to justify caching (>50ms without cache)
assumptions.note: Load test on real hardware showed 12ms without cache. Original measurement was from dev environment with network overhead.

assumptions.text: Cache invalidation on task updates is simple enough to implement correctly

affects.note: Task service calls cache layer before DB

decisions.question: How should tasks be associated with projects?
decisions.notes: Originally used a single project_id FK on the task table — simple and fast to implement. Superseded when product added multi-project tasks as a requirement. See D-005.

options.label: Single project_id foreign key on task
Many-to-many join table
options.rejection_reason: Over-engineered for MVP; product confirmed tasks belong to exactly one project

assumptions.text: A task belongs to exactly one project
assumptions.note: Product requirement changed: enterprise users need to tag tasks across multiple projects

decisions.question: How should tasks be associated with multiple projects?
decisions.notes: D-004 assumption broke when enterprise requirement came in. Went with a join table this time — the originally-rejected option. The initial rejection reason (over-engineered for MVP) is no longer valid now that multi-project is confirmed. Implementation not yet started.

options.label: Array column of project_ids on task
options.rejection_reason: Cannot enforce FK constraints; makes cross-project queries harder

assumptions.text: Tasks can belong to many projects but the typical count is low (<10)
Queries will filter by project more often than by task

affects.note: New TaskProject model, remove project_id from Task
Data migration needed to move existing FKs to join table
All task queries need updating to join through new table
UI already supports multiple project badges; API shape change needed
